headline: Expat Salary Floor Doubles to RM20K — The Talent Equation Nobody Solves
context: From 1 June 2026, Employment Pass Category I minimum rises from RM10,000 to RM20,000. Category III capped at 5 years with mandatory succession planning. The policy aims to prioritise local talent, but the talent pipeline, brain drain, and enforcement gaps remain unaddressed.

cards.big: Malaysia doubles the expat salary floor to RM20,000. The message: hire Malaysians first.
cards.sub: RM10,000 was too low and undercut locals. A higher floor can attract quality investment and force knowledge transfer. But is the system ready?

cards.big: Semiconductor target: 60,000 workers by 2030. Pipeline: 3,000 graduates/year — many leave for Singapore within five years.
cards.sub: Malaysia loses skilled professionals to neighbours offering higher pay and clearer meritocracy. Raising the price of foreign replacements doesn't fix why Malaysians leave.

cards.big: GLCs and MNCs absorb the RM20K floor easily. SMEs and startups — the actual growth engine — cannot.
cards.sub: A Penang semiconductor startup now faces double the floor for a foreign engineer. Succession planning is mandatory on paper but rarely enforced. The burden falls hardest on firms least able to bear it.

cards.big: Malaysia's new RM20K floor exceeds Singapore's EP minimum of SGD5,000 (~RM16,600).
cards.sub: Multinationals choosing between Penang and Batam now see Malaysia as the more expensive jurisdiction. Thailand and Vietnam offer alternatives with lower barriers.

cards.big: The real talent equation: pipeline quality, brain drain, and enforcement. This policy addresses none — it adjusts the price tag on a problem that is not about price.
Protecting Malaysian jobs is right. But protection without pipeline investment produces paper compliance, not specialists. The ladder must come before the bar.
